title: Asset allocation as a decision, not a formula
subtitle: There is no universally correct asset allocation. There is only the allocation that is correct for your circumstances at this stage of your life.

body.content: Rules of thumb for asset allocation — 60/40, 100 minus your age in bonds — are starting points, not answers. They are useful for illustrating a principle (as you age, reduce risk) but they ignore the variables that actually determine the right allocation for any individual: income stability, time horizon, liabilities, existing assets, and behavioral risk tolerance.
The variables that determine your allocation

items: Time horizon: longer horizons can absorb more volatility and should hold more equities. Shorter horizons demand more stability.

Income stability: a tenured professional with predictable income can treat their future earnings as a bond-like asset, supporting a more equity-heavy portfolio. A self-employed person with variable income may need more portfolio stability to compensate.
Liability structure: significant near-term obligations (mortgage, education, care costs) reduce investable capital and increase the need for liquidity.
Existing wealth: high accumulated wealth relative to expected future needs supports taking less risk — the goal of preservation matters more than growth.

body.label: The allocation decision process
body.content: Start with your goals. Derive the return you need to meet them. Find the lowest-risk portfolio that can reasonably deliver that return over your time horizon. Do not reach for more risk than required — there is no reward for unnecessary risk-taking.

Revisit allocation decisions at major life transitions: career change, marriage, children, inheritance, approaching retirement. The right allocation at 35 is not the right allocation at 55 — not because the rules changed, but because your circumstances did.
Asset allocation is the most important investment decision you will make. It should be derived from your specific circumstances — not from a rule of thumb that knows nothing about your life.
